questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with a two-day history of fever, rigors, and left flank pain. She reports urinary frequency and dysuria. On examination, temperature 38.9°C, left costovertebral angle tenderness. Urine dipstick shows leucocytes and nitrites positive. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Fever, flank pain, and urinary symptoms with dipstick findings point to upper UTI. Lower UTI lacks systemic features; renal colic has hematuria but no fever; appendicitis is right-sided; PID has lower abdominal pain.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents with confusion and falls. His daughter reports that he has been incontinent of urine at home. He is febrile at 38.2°C, tachycardic, and has mild suprapubic tenderness. Urine dipstick shows leucocytes and nitrites. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Elderly may present with confusion and falls; fever, urinary symptoms, and dipstick suggest upper UTI. Lower UTI doesn't usually cause systemic symptoms; constipation doesn't cause fever; DKA would have hyperglycemia; urosepsis is a complication but diagnosis here is pyelonephritis (source).

questions.stem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 30 weeks gestation presents with high fever (39.5°C), rigors, and right loin pain. She has vomiting and dehydration. On examination, right renal angle tenderness, heart rate 110/min, BP 95/60. Urine dipstick: leucocytes, nitrites, and blood. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Classic triad of fever, rigors, loin pain in pregnancy points to pyelonephritis. Placental abruption presents with vaginal bleeding and abdominal pain; renal colic is not febrile; chorioamnionitis involves uterine tenderness; PE has respiratory symptoms.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old renal transplant recipient (on tacrolimus and prednisolone) presents with fever, graft site tenderness, and oliguria. He had a urinary tract infection treated two weeks ago. Temperature 38.5°C, white cell count elevated, serum creatinine has risen by 30% from baseline. Urine culture grows E. coli. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Fever, graft tenderness, and recent UTI with same organism suggest graft pyelonephritis. Rejection is less likely with fever and positive culture; CNI toxicity doesn't cause fever; BK virus nephropathy often asymptomatic; stenosis has hypertension.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with a history of recurrent UTIs due to bladder outlet obstruction presents with fever, rigors, and right loin pain. He has frequency and dysuria. On examination, temperature 39.0°C, right renal angle tenderness. Urine dipstick positive for leucocytes and nitrites. Digital rectal examination reveals a moderately enlarged but non-tender prostate. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Flank pain, fever, urinary symptoms with non-tender prostate point to pyelonephritis. Prostatitis requires tender prostate; lower UTI lacks systemic features; RCC presents with hematuria or mass; epididymitis has scrotal pain.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman presents with a 2-day history of fever, right loin pain, and dysuria. There is no known drug allergy. Observations: temperature 38.5°C, pulse 95 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg. Urine dipstick shows nitrites and leukocytes. Which single investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Non-contrast CT of the kidneys, ureters and bladder
questions.explanation: Midstream urine culture and sensitivity is the gold standard to confirm acute pyelonephritis and guide antibiotic therapy. Urine dipstick is a screening test but not confirmatory. Imaging (ultrasound, CT) is reserved for complicated cases or poor response. Blood cultures may be helpful if sepsis is suspected but do not confirm the renal infection.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 24 weeks gestation presents with fever, left loin pain, and vomiting. Temperature 39.0°C, pulse 100 bpm, BP 100/70 mmHg. Urine culture from the community is growing Gram-negative rods. She is admitted for intravenous antibiotics. Which single investigation is most appropriate next to evaluate for an underlying structural abnormality?
questions.explanation: Renal ultrasound is the first-line imaging in pregnancy to exclude obstruction or hydronephrosis, as it is safe and avoids ionising radiation. CT urogram is contraindicated in pregnancy. MRI urogram is an alternative if ultrasound is inadequate but is not first-line. Cystoscopy and MCU are not indicated acutely.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes presents with fever, left loin pain, and confusion. Observations: temperature 39.5°C, pulse 120 bpm, BP 90/60 mmHg. Urine dipstick shows nitrites, leukocytes and blood. He is started on broad-spectrum antibiotics and fluid resuscitation. Which single investigation is most appropriate to confirm a renal abscess or emphysematous pyelonephritis?
options: Non-contrast CT of the kidneys, ureters and bladder
Magnetic resonance imaging of the abdomen
CT abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast
questions.explanation: Contrast-enhanced CT is the gold standard for diagnosing emphysematous pyelonephritis and renal abscesses, as it accurately demonstrates gas and fluid collections. Ultrasound may miss small gas pockets. Non-contrast CT can detect gas but is less sensitive for abscesses. Plain X-ray and MRI are less reliable or slower.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with recurrent urinary tract infections presents with acute right loin pain and fever. She has a history of nephrolithiasis. Observations: temperature 38.0°C, pulse 100 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg. Urine culture is pending. Which single investigation is most appropriate to confirm a suspected obstructing ureteric stone causing pyelonephritis?
options: Non-contrast CT of the kidneys, ureters and bladder
questions.explanation: Non-contrast CT (CT KUB) is the most sensitive and specific test for detecting ureteric stones, particularly in the acute setting with obstruction and infection. Ultrasound may miss small stones, especially in the mid-ureter. IVU and plain X-ray are less sensitive. Contrast CT may obscure stones or delay diagnosis.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man with benign prostatic hyperplasia presents with recurrent fevers and left loin pain. He has had three episodes of pyelonephritis in the past year. Urine culture is positive for Escherichia coli. Which single investigation is most appropriate to identify an underlying structural abnormality of the urinary tract?
options: Magnetic resonance imaging of the kidneys
questions.explanation: CT urogram is the investigation of choice for evaluating the entire urinary tract in recurrent pyelonephritis, as it can detect stones, tumours, and anatomical abnormalities. Ultrasound is less sensitive for ureteric pathology. MRI is not first-line. Cystoscopy is reserved for suspected bladder abnormalities. DMSA assesses renal scarring but not acute structural causes.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and vomiting. Urine dip shows nitrites and leukocytes. She has no known allergies. eGFR >60. She is haemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate first-line oral antibiotic for acute pyelonephritis according to UK guidelines?
questions.explanation: Co-amoxiclav is first-line oral treatment for pyelonephritis per UK guidelines due to its adequate renal penetration and spectrum. Nitrofurantoin is ineffective for pyelonephritis as it does not achieve therapeutic levels in renal parenchyma. Trimethoprim and ciprofloxacin are alternatives if co-amoxiclav is unsuitable (e.g., allergy), but not first-line.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with diabetes presents with septic shock secondary to pyelonephritis (temperature 39.5°C, BP 85/50, lactate 4). He has a catheter. Blood cultures pending. What is the most appropriate initial IV antibiotic regimen?
questions.explanation: In septic shock from a urinary source, IV piperacillin/tazobactam is recommended as it provides broad-spectrum coverage including Pseudomonas, resistant Gram-negatives, and anaerobes. Ceftriaxone or co-amoxiclav may be used in non-severe cases but are insufficient for sepsis with shock. Gentamicin monotherapy is not guideline-recommended as initial empirical therapy.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman with acute pyelonephritis has persistent fever and flank pain after 48 hours of IV antibiotics. CT shows a 7 mm obstructing stone at the PUJ. What is the most appropriate definitive management in addition to continuing antibiotics?
questions.explanation: In infected obstructed kidney, urgent decompression is mandatory. Ureteric stent placement is the first-line drainage method as it is less invasive than nephrostomy and allows prompt relief of obstruction. ESWL and ureteroscopy are contraindicated in acute infection. Continuing antibiotics alone risks sepsis.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old primigravida at 28 weeks presents with pyelonephritis. She is febrile but stable. Which one of the following antibiotics is CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy?
questions.explanation: Ciprofloxacin is contraindicated in pregnancy due to risk of arthropathy in the fetus. Nitrofurantoin is avoided in the third trimester (risk of haemolytic anaemia) but is not absolutely contraindicated; it is sometimes used in early pregnancy. Gentamicin is used with caution but not contraindicated. Ceftriaxone and co-amoxiclav are safe.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman has recurrent pyelonephritis. She has completed a 7-day course of co-amoxiclav. Urine culture grew E. coli resistant to co-amoxiclav and trimethoprim but sensitive to nitrofurantoin and ciprofloxacin. She is now asymptomatic. What is the most appropriate definitive management to prevent recurrence?
options: A urinary antiseptic such as methenamine hippurate
questions.explanation: For recurrent UTIs, low-dose antibiotic prophylaxis (e.g., nitrofurantoin 50–100 mg at night) is recommended to reduce recurrence. Ciprofloxacin as a treatment course would address an active infection but not prevent recurrence. Methenamine hippurate is an alternative but less effective and not first-line in this setting. Prolonging co-amoxiclav is inappropriate given resistance.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of dysuria, frequency, and fevers. She has right flank tenderness and a temperature of 38.5°C. Heart rate 90 bpm, blood pressure 120/80 mmHg. Urine dipstick shows positive nitrites and leucocytes. She has no significant past medical history. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Send urine for culture and sensitivity
Start oral trimethoprim 200 mg twice daily
questions.explanation: In an otherwise healthy, haemodynamically stable woman with suspected uncomplicated pyelonephritis, NICE recommends starting empirical oral antibiotics (e.g., trimethoprim) without delay. Sending a urine culture is important but should not postpone treatment.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with a history of recurrent UTIs presents with fever, rigors, and right loin pain. On examination, temperature 39°C, heart rate 110 bpm, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg. Urine dipstick shows positive leucocytes and blood. He is nauseated but not vomiting. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Send blood cultures and start oral ciprofloxacin
questions.explanation: The patient has sepsis (tachycardia, fever) secondary to pyelonephritis. The immediate priority is to administer intravenous antibiotics (e.g., co-amoxiclav) within 1 hour. IV fluids are also important but can be given concurrently; oral antibiotics are insufficient in sepsis.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes presents with confusion, fever, and rigors. She is hypotensive (80/50 mmHg), heart rate 120 bpm, temperature 39.5°C. She has left flank tenderness and oliguria. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Insert a central line for CVP monitoring
Give a 500 mL intravenous fluid bolus of crystalloid
questions.explanation: This patient is in septic shock. The first priority is to restore perfusion with a rapid fluid bolus. Antibiotics should be given as soon as possible after cultures, but fluid resuscitation is the immediate life-saving intervention.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman admitted with pyelonephritis has been on intravenous co-amoxiclav for 3 days. She remains febrile (38.5°C) with persistent right flank pain. Her blood cultures grew E. coli sensitive to co-amoxiclav. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Arrange a renal ultrasound to look for abscess
questions.explanation: Failure to respond after 72 hours of appropriate antibiotics suggests a complication such as a renal or perinephric abscess. Renal ultrasound is the first-line imaging to assess for this. Changing antibiotics is not indicated if sensitivity is confirmed.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old pregnant woman at 20 weeks gestation presents with a 1-day history of dysuria, fever, and left flank pain. She is nauseated but not vomiting. Temperature 38.5°C, heart rate 95 bpm, blood pressure 100/60 mmHg. Urine dipstick positive for leucocytes and nitrites. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer to the obstetric team for outpatient management
Arrange a renal ultrasound to exclude obstructive uropathy
Send urine for culture and prescribe oral cephalexin
Start oral nitrofurantoin 100 mg twice daily
Admit for intravenous co-amoxiclav 1.2 g three times daily
questions.explanation: Pyelonephritis in pregnancy is a complicated infection requiring hospital admission and intravenous antibiotics (e.g., co-amoxiclav). Oral therapy (e.g., nitrofurantoin) is not sufficient for pyelonephritis. Outpatient management is inappropriate due to risk of maternal and fetal complications.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman presents with fever, rigors, right loin pain, and dysuria. She has a temperature of 38.8°C, heart rate 105 bpm, blood pressure 100/70 mmHg, and is able to take oral fluids. Which single finding would prompt immediate referral for hospital admission?
questions.explanation: A systolic blood pressure <90 mmHg indicates sepsis and a need for urgent escalation (NICE shock criteria). While high fever and vomiting may suggest severity, hypotension is the clearest red flag for shock requiring immediate hospital care.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with a history of BPH presents with fever, dysuria, and left flank pain. On examination, he is febrile at 38.5°C, tender at the left costovertebral angle. Which examination finding would be a red flag for acute urinary obstruction requiring urgent urological intervention?
questions.explanation: A palpable bladder indicates urinary retention from outflow obstruction (e.g., enlarged prostate, stone), which can precipitate urosepsis and requires urgent catheterisation or decompression. Suprapubic tenderness is nonspecific; flank ecchymosis suggests trauma.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman at 28 weeks gestation presents with fever, right flank pain, and urine dip positive for nitrites and leucocytes. She is otherwise stable. Which symptom should alert you to a potential complication of pregnancy specific to pyelonephritis?
questions.explanation: Regular abdominal tightening (contractions) suggests preterm labour, a well-known complication of acute pyelonephritis in pregnancy. Headache and visual disturbances could indicate preeclampsia but are not specific pyelonephritis complications.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes presents with fever, vomiting, and severe left flank pain for 2 days. She appears toxic. On examination, temperature 39.5°C, HR 120, BP 85/50. Which clinical sign would raise suspicion for emphysematous pyelonephritis?
options: Crepitus on palpation of the left flank
questions.explanation: Crepitus (subcutaneous emphysema) over the flank signifies gas in the soft tissues from a gas-forming organism, a hallmark of emphysematous pyelonephritis—a surgical emergency. Marked tenderness is typical but not specific.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old patient with pyelonephritis develops sudden confusion, tachypnoea, and hypotension. Which investigation finding is most consistent with the development of acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) as a complication of sepsis?
options: Arterial blood gas showing metabolic alkalosis
Chest X-ray showing bilateral interstitial and airspace opacities
questions.explanation: Bilateral diffuse opacities on chest X-ray are characteristic of ARDS, a life-threatening complication of severe sepsis. Unilateral consolidation suggests pneumonia; CT head changes are not typical for ARDS.
